title: Observation of Universal Spectral Moments and the Dynamic Dispersive-to-Proliferative Transition
authors: Jia-Xin Zhong, Chang Shu, Nan Cheng, Jee Woo Kim, Kai Zhang, Kai Sun, Yun Jing
abstract: In non-Hermitian systems, spectra can be maximally boundary-sensitive, yet bulk physics need not be. Here we experimentally show that spectral moments provide boundary-robust bulk observables in finite non-Hermitian lattices, even when the spectra undergo dramatic geometry-dependent reshaping due to the skin effect. Using a unified acoustic platform with full spectral reconstruction and time-domain access, we probe one-, two- and three-dimensional lattices and demonstrate that spectral moments remain nearly invariant across distinct boundary geometries while the corresponding complex spectra differ strongly. To connect the thermodynamic theorem to realistic finite systems, we develop a loop-counting theory that identifies the physical origin of finite-size deviations in terms of missing boundary loops, quantitatively captures the corrections, and predicts a scaling law, which we verify experimentally. Beyond acoustic spectroscopy, we reveal a counterintuitive dynamical consequence of moment invariance: a dispersive-to-proliferative bulk transition governed by bulk moment structure rather than spectral boundary sensitivity. As a result, local bulk dynamics can remain stable (dispersive) even in a $\mathcal{PT}$-broken spectral regime, challenging the conventional expectation that $\mathcal{PT}$ breaking necessarily implies feedback-induced dynamical instability (proliferation) through exponentially amplifying spectral components. These results establish spectral moments as practical bulk descriptors for finite non-Hermitian matter and open a route to extracting and controlling intrinsic bulk behavior in realistic wave-based non-Hermitian devices.
summary: The paper shows that while the energy spectra of non-Hermitian systems change drastically with boundary conditions, their spectral moments remain nearly invariant and reflect the true bulk physics. By using an acoustic platform, the authors verify a universal scaling law for finite-size errors and discover a new dynamical phase transition driven by bulk properties.
main_problem: How to characterize intrinsic bulk physics in non-Hermitian systems when the Non-Hermitian Skin Effect (NHSE) causes the complex spectrum to be extremely sensitive to boundary conditions and geometry.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that spectral moments are boundary-robust descriptors of bulk physics and establish a universal scaling law for finite-size deviations. They also reveal a dynamical transition from dispersive to proliferative regimes governed by bulk moments rather than boundary-sensitive spectra.
method: The study uses an active acoustic metamaterial platform for full spectral reconstruction and time-domain wave-packet tracking. They employ Green's-function-based spectroscopy and a loop-counting theory to analyze 1D, 2D, and 3D lattices.
model_or_system: A unified acoustic metamaterial platform implementing non-Hermitian tight-binding models in 1D, 2D, and 3D. The system uses electro-acoustic feedback loops to control non-reciprocal hopping amplitudes and on-site potentials.
key_observables: Complex energy spectra, spectral moments (up to 10th order), relative deviation between boundary conditions, and wave-packet amplitude evolution.
important_parameters: Boundary coupling strength (g), system size (L or N), moment order (m), non-reciprocity/non-Hermiticity parameter (gamma), and hopping amplitudes.
main_assumptions: The loop-counting theory assumes a regime where on-site potentials dominate over off-diagonal terms (|H_ii| >> |H_{i != j}|) for certain scaling predictions.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Concept of spectral moments, loop-counting logic, and acoustic lattice photo. Fig 2: 1D lattice spectra under varying boundary conditions, moment invariance, and finite-size scaling. Fig 3: 2D and 3D lattice implementations and higher-dimensional scaling.
paper_structure: Introduction of the boundary sensitivity paradox, presentation of the acoustic platform, demonstration of spectral moment invariance in 1D, derivation and verification of finite-size scaling laws, extension to 2D and 3D geometries, and investigation of the dynamical dispersive-to-proliferative transition.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a new way to extract intrinsic bulk properties from finite-size non-Hermitian systems, decoupling local stability from boundary-induced spectral fluctuations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Boundary-sensitive spectra versus boundary-robust spectral moments, and the acoustic lattice platform. a, For the same bulk non-Hermitian lattice Hamiltonian at finite size L, the complex spectrum {En} reshapes drastically under different boundary geometries (PBC torus versus OBC P- and M-shaped domains), whereas the per-site spectral moments Mα,m(L) are nearly boundary inde- pendent. The residual geometry dependence scales as a universal finite-size correction O(m2/L), and Mα,m(L) →Mm(∞) as L →∞. b, Loop-counting picture for the finite-size deviation. The dashed curve partitions the domain into a bulk region Ωb and a boundary shell Ωe of thickness ∼m/2 (in hop distance). For a...

Figure 2: Observation of spectral moments and finite-size scaling in a 1D non-Hermitian lat- tice. a, Tight-binding schematic of the 1D non-Hermitian lattice. The Bloch Hamiltonian is H(k) = κ+e−2ik + κ−e2ik + 2κ cos k + ω0. A generalized boundary condition (GBC) continu- ously interpolates between open boundary condition (OBC) and periodic boundary condition (PBC) via HGBC = HOBC + g(HPBC −HOBC). Boundary sites (sites 1 and N) are highlighted in dark brown. b, Measured (top) and simulated (bottom) complex spectra under GBCs with different boundary coupling strengths g (color coded). The experiment uses g = 0, 0.1, 1, 2, while the simulation addi- tionally includes a near-OBC case g =...

Figure 3: Observation of spectral moments in higher-dimensional lattices. a, Tight-binding schematic of the 2D reciprocal non-Hermitian lattice. The Bloch Hamiltonian is H(kx, ky) = 2κx cos kx+2κy cos(kx + ky)+ω0. Parameters are: κx = 2i Hz, κy = 4 Hz, and ω0 = 1040 Hz−6i Hz. b–d, The top panel shows three different real-space implementations of a under PBCs in both x and y directions: b a regular 8×8 square lattice, and c, d 9×9 square lattices with a few removed sites (to form an irregular boundary) to show the letter c ‘P’ and d ‘M’. The bottom panel in b–d shows the measured (red squares) and simulated (blue dots) energy spectra. e, Spectral moments up to the 10th order obtained from...

Figure 4: Observation of the dynamic dispersive-to-proliferative phase transition. a, Tight-binding schematic of the 1D non-Hermitian PT-symmetric lattice. The Bloch Hamiltonian is H(k) = 2α(1 − γ)e−3ik +(1−γ)2e−2ik +2α(1+γ)e−ik +2α(1−γ)eik +(1+γ)2e2ik +2α(1+γ)e3ik. b–d, Results for the three representative regimes: b PT -symmetric (γ = 0), c dispersive (γ = 0.13), and d proliferative (γ = 0.45). In each of b–d, the top panel shows the measured OBC spectra (red dots), the simulated OBC spectra (blue dots), and the theoretical PBC spectra (black curves). The middle and bottom panels show the measured and simulated spatiotemporal evolution of the wave-function amplitude for an initial...
